name: Cognitive Psychology
description: An in-depth exploration of mental processes including attention, perception, memory, language, problem solving, and decision making. Students examine classic experiments and contemporary research to understand how the mind organizes, stores, and retrieves information.

modules.title: History of Cognitive Psychology
modules.objectives: Trace the cognitive revolution from behaviorism to information-processing models; identify key figures including Neisser, Miller, and Broadbent.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Ch 7.1: What is Cognition
modules.videoNote: CrashCourse Psychology #2 — Psychological Research & the Cognitive Revolution
modules.video2Note: The History of Cognitive Psychology — Overview Lecture
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — History of Psychology Practice
modules.assignment: Write a timeline essay tracing the shift from behaviorism to cognitive psychology. Include at least four key figures and their contributions. (350 words)

modules.title: Perception & Attention
modules.objectives: Explain selective, divided, and sustained attention; describe bottleneck theories; apply findings from the cocktail party effect and change blindness.
modules.videoNote: Daniel Simons — The Invisible Gorilla (Inattentional Blindness Demo)
modules.assignment: Conduct the invisible gorilla attention experiment informally with 5 participants. Report your findings and explain using attentional theories. (300 words)

modules.title: Memory Systems
modules.objectives: Distinguish sensory, short-term, and long-term memory; compare the Atkinson-Shiffrin model with Baddeley's working memory model.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Ch 8.1: How Memory Functions
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Memory Encoding & Storage
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Memory Encoding & Storage Practice
modules.assignment: Create a diagram comparing the Atkinson-Shiffrin model with Baddeley's working memory model. Annotate each component's function and capacity. (with 200-word explanation)

modules.title: Working Memory
modules.objectives: Describe Baddeley's components of working memory (phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, episodic buffer, central executive); explain its role in reasoning and comprehension.
modules.video2Note: Working Memory and Learning — Educational Overview
modules.assignment: Test your own working memory capacity using a digit-span task. Compare your results to published norms and explain implications for learning. (300 words)

modules.title: Long-term Memory
modules.objectives: Differentiate explicit (declarative: episodic and semantic) from implicit (procedural, priming) memory; explain consolidation and the role of the hippocampus.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Ch 8.2: Brain & Long-term Memory
modules.videoNote: Types of Long-term Memory — Explicit vs. Implicit
modules.assignment: Give three examples each of episodic, semantic, and procedural memories from your own life. Analyze how each was encoded and what retrieval cues help access them. (350 words)

modules.title: Forgetting
modules.objectives: Explain Ebbinghaus's forgetting curve; distinguish decay theory from interference theory; describe retrieval failure and motivated forgetting.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Ch 8.3–8.4: Memory Storage & Forgetting
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Forgetting & Amnesia
modules.assignment: Apply spaced repetition principles to a list of 20 vocabulary words. Track your retention over 3 days. Analyze how your forgetting curve compares to Ebbinghaus's. (300 words)

modules.title: Mental Imagery
modules.objectives: Describe mental rotation and image scanning research; evaluate whether mental images are pictorial or propositional; apply imagery to memory enhancement.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Ch 7.1: Concepts & Mental Imagery
modules.videoNote: Mental Rotation and Spatial Reasoning — Cognitive Psychology
modules.assignment: Take a mental rotation test online and reflect on your performance. What does spatial ability research tell us about individual differences in mental imagery? (300 words)

modules.title: Language & Thought
modules.objectives: Explain the structure of language (phonemes, morphemes, syntax, semantics); evaluate the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis; describe Chomsky's universal grammar.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Language & Thought
modules.assignment: Research one study on linguistic relativity (e.g., color perception across languages). Summarize the study and discuss whether language shapes thought or vice versa. (350 words)

quizQuestions.question: The cognitive revolution emerged primarily as a reaction against:
quizQuestions.answer: Behaviorism
quizQuestions.explanation: The cognitive revolution in the 1950s-60s was a rejection of behaviorism's exclusive focus on observable behavior.

quizQuestions.question: George Miller's famous 1956 paper identified short-term memory capacity as approximately:
quizQuestions.answer: 7 items (plus or minus 2)
quizQuestions.explanation: Miller's 'Magic Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two' described the limited capacity of short-term memory.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The cognitive revolution emerged primarily as a reaction against:" is: Behaviorism.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Behaviorism is correct.

quizQuestions.question: The cocktail party effect demonstrates:
options: Selective attention to relevant stimuli in a noisy environment
The inability to process two conversations simultaneously
quizQuestions.answer: Selective attention to relevant stimuli in a noisy environment
quizQuestions.explanation: The cocktail party effect shows we can selectively attend to our name even in a crowded, noisy setting.

quizQuestions.question: Change blindness refers to:
options: The inability to perceive color changes
Failure to notice large changes in a scene when attention is elsewhere
A medical condition affecting peripheral vision
quizQuestions.answer: Failure to notice large changes in a scene when attention is elsewhere
quizQuestions.explanation: Change blindness demonstrates that we do not automatically encode all aspects of a visual scene.

quizQuestions.question: The inattentional blindness experiment by Simons and Chabris showed:
options: Observers miss unexpected objects when focused on a primary task
Vision is processed entirely in parallel
quizQuestions.answer: Observers miss unexpected objects when focused on a primary task
quizQuestions.explanation: When focused on counting basketball passes, many participants failed to notice a person in a gorilla suit walking through.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The cocktail party effect demonstrates:" is: Selective attention to relevant stimuli in a noisy environment.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Selective attention to relevant stimuli in a noisy environment is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Sensory memory holds information for approximately:
options: Less than a second to a few seconds
quizQuestions.answer: Less than a second to a few seconds
quizQuestions.explanation: Sensory memory (iconic and echoic) holds exact copies of sensory input for very brief durations before it decays.

quizQuestions.question: The Atkinson-Shiffrin model is also known as the:
quizQuestions.answer: Multi-store model
quizQuestions.explanation: Atkinson and Shiffrin's 1968 model proposed three distinct memory stores: sensory, short-term, and long-term.

quizQuestions.question: Rehearsal in the Atkinson-Shiffrin model serves to:
options: Transfer information from STM to LTM
quizQuestions.answer: Transfer information from STM to LTM
quizQuestions.explanation: Maintenance rehearsal recirculates information in STM; elaborative rehearsal encodes it into LTM.

quizQuestions.question: The phonological loop in Baddeley's working memory model handles:
quizQuestions.answer: Verbal and auditory information
quizQuestions.explanation: The phonological loop temporarily stores and rehearses verbal/auditory information (like repeating a phone number).

quizQuestions.question: The central executive in Baddeley's model functions as:
options: The sole source of memory capacity
quizQuestions.answer: An attentional controller coordinating subsystems
quizQuestions.explanation: The central executive directs attention and controls the slave systems (phonological loop and visuospatial sketchpad).

quizQuestions.question: The word-length effect demonstrates that:
options: Working memory capacity is affected by how long words take to say
Short words are harder to chunk
Rehearsal does not help working memory
quizQuestions.answer: Working memory capacity is affected by how long words take to say
quizQuestions.explanation: Longer words take more time to rehearse, reducing the number that can be held in the phonological loop.

quizQuestions.question: The episodic buffer in Baddeley's model was added to account for:
options: The integration of information across modalities into coherent episodes
quizQuestions.answer: The integration of information across modalities into coherent episodes
quizQuestions.explanation: Added in 2000, the episodic buffer integrates information from the phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, and LTM into unified episodes.

quizQuestions.question: Episodic memory contains memories of:
quizQuestions.answer: Personal autobiographical events
quizQuestions.explanation: Episodic memory stores personally experienced events with their temporal and spatial context.

quizQuestions.question: Priming is best classified as:
quizQuestions.answer: Implicit memory
quizQuestions.explanation: Priming — a prior exposure facilitating later processing — is an implicit, unconscious form of memory.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Episodic memory contains memories of:" is: Personal autobiographical events.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Personal autobiographical events is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Priming is best classified as:" is: Explicit memory.
quizQuestions.answer: False
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Implicit memory.

quizQuestions.question: Ebbinghaus's forgetting curve shows that forgetting is:
options: Rapid initially then slowing over time
quizQuestions.answer: Rapid initially then slowing over time
quizQuestions.explanation: Ebbinghaus's forgetting curve shows steep initial forgetting that decelerates as time passes.

quizQuestions.question: Proactive interference occurs when:
options: New memories interfere with old ones
Old memories interfere with new learning
quizQuestions.answer: Old memories interfere with new learning
quizQuestions.explanation: Proactive interference: old information makes it harder to learn or remember new information.

quizQuestions.question: Retrograde amnesia results in:
options: Loss of memories prior to brain injury
quizQuestions.answer: Loss of memories prior to brain injury
quizQuestions.explanation: Retrograde amnesia causes loss of memories formed before the injury, while anterograde amnesia affects new memory formation.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Ebbinghaus's forgetting curve shows that forgetting is:" is: Rapid initially then slowing over time.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Rapid initially then slowing over time is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Shepard and Metzler's mental rotation experiments showed that:
options: Images are stored in verbal codes
Rotation time increases linearly with the angle of rotation
Visual imagery uses different brain areas than perception
quizQuestions.answer: Rotation time increases linearly with the angle of rotation
quizQuestions.explanation: Response time increased proportionally with the degree of rotation, suggesting people mentally rotate images in a continuous, analog way.

quizQuestions.question: The pictorial view of mental imagery (Kosslyn) proposes that:
options: Images are stored as abstract propositions
Mental images are depictive, spatial representations
Images are reconstructed from semantic memory
quizQuestions.answer: Mental images are depictive, spatial representations
quizQuestions.explanation: Kosslyn argued mental images are quasi-pictorial, activating visual cortex areas similar to actual perception.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Shepard and Metzler's mental rotation experiments showed that:" is: Rotation time increases linearly with the angle of rotation.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Rotation time increases linearly with the angle of rotation is correct.

quizQuestions.question: A morpheme is best defined as:
options: The smallest unit of sound in a language
The smallest unit of meaning in a language
The meaning of a word in context
quizQuestions.answer: The smallest unit of meaning in a language
quizQuestions.explanation: Morphemes are the smallest linguistic units that carry meaning (e.g., 'un-', 'happy', '-ness').

quizQuestions.question: Chomsky's universal grammar hypothesis states that:
options: All languages share the same vocabulary roots
Humans have an innate capacity for language structure
Only spoken language follows grammar rules
quizQuestions.answer: Humans have an innate capacity for language structure
quizQuestions.explanation: Chomsky proposed that all humans are biologically equipped with a universal grammar framework for language acquisition.

quizQuestions.question: The strong Sapir-Whorf hypothesis would mean that:
options: Language has no effect on thought
Speakers of different languages perceive reality differently due to their language
Thought develops before language in children
quizQuestions.answer: Speakers of different languages perceive reality differently due to their language
quizQuestions.explanation: The strong Sapir-Whorf hypothesis posits that the language we speak determines how we think and perceive the world.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "A morpheme is best defined as:" is: The smallest unit of meaning in a language.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. The smallest unit of meaning in a language is correct.

questions.question: The cognitive revolution was primarily a reaction against which school of psychology?
questions.answer: Behaviorism
questions.explanation: The cognitive revolution in the 1950s-60s rejected behaviorism's exclusive focus on observable behavior, reintroducing mental processes as scientific subjects.

questions.question: George Miller's 'magic number' for short-term memory capacity is approximately:
questions.answer: 7 items (plus or minus 2)
questions.explanation: Miller found that short-term memory holds approximately 7 (plus or minus 2) chunks of information.

questions.question: The cocktail party effect demonstrates:
options: Inability to attend to any background noise
Selective attention to one's name in a noisy environment
Parallel processing of all auditory stimuli
The limit of working memory capacity
questions.answer: Selective attention to one's name in a noisy environment
questions.explanation: The cocktail party effect shows we selectively filter relevant stimuli (like our name) even while attending to other things.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "The central executive in Baddeley's working memory model serves as:" is: An attentional controller coordinating the subsystems.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. An attentional controller coordinating the subsystems is correct.

questions.question: Sperling's partial report procedure showed that iconic memory:
options: Holds more information than can be fully recalled
questions.answer: Holds more information than can be fully recalled
questions.explanation: By cuing participants to report specific rows after stimulus offset, Sperling showed iconic memory holds more than participants can fully report.

questions.question: Encoding specificity states that recall is best when:
questions.answer: Retrieval context matches encoding context
questions.explanation: Tulving's encoding specificity: memory is strongest when the conditions at retrieval match those present during encoding.

questions.question: Shepard and Metzler's mental rotation studies suggested that mental images are:
options: Not processed in the visual system
questions.answer: Analog and spatially represented
questions.explanation: Rotation time increased linearly with angle of rotation, suggesting mental images behave like physical objects being rotated.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Functional fixedness is an obstacle to problem solving because it:" is: Leads to over-reliance on algorithms.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Prevents seeing novel uses for familiar objects.

questions.question: Chomsky's universal grammar proposes that:
options: All languages share identical grammar rules
Children have an innate predisposition to acquire language structure
questions.answer: Children have an innate predisposition to acquire language structure
questions.explanation: Chomsky proposed a biologically innate language acquisition device that provides children with a universal grammar framework.

questions.question: The case of patient H.M. demonstrated that the hippocampus is critical for:
questions.answer: Forming new explicit memories
questions.explanation: After bilateral hippocampal removal, H.M. could not form new declarative (explicit) memories.

questions.question: Ebbinghaus's forgetting curve shows that forgetting is:
options: Most rapid shortly after learning then slowing
Faster for emotionally neutral material only
questions.answer: Most rapid shortly after learning then slowing
questions.explanation: Ebbinghaus showed that the most forgetting occurs shortly after learning, with the rate slowing as time progresses.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Kahneman's System 1 thinking is best described as:" is: Fast, automatic, and intuitive.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. Fast, automatic, and intuitive is correct.

questions.question: Deliberate practice differs from ordinary practice in that it:
options: Focuses on stretching abilities beyond current limits with specific feedback
questions.answer: Focuses on stretching abilities beyond current limits with specific feedback
questions.explanation: Deliberate practice targets specific weaknesses at the edge of one's competence with immediate corrective feedback.

questions.question: Piaget's formal operational stage is distinguished by:
options: Ability to think abstractly and hypothetically
questions.answer: Ability to think abstractly and hypothetically
questions.explanation: During formal operations (age 12+), adolescents can reason about hypothetical scenarios, abstract concepts, and systematic logic.

questions.question: A connectionist (PDP) model represents knowledge as:
options: Activation patterns distributed across interconnected units
questions.answer: Activation patterns distributed across interconnected units
questions.explanation: Connectionist models represent knowledge as distributed patterns of activation across networks of simple processing units.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "The testing effect demonstrates that:" is: Re-reading is the most effective study strategy.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Retrieval practice produces stronger long-term retention than passive review.

questions.question: Loftus's misinformation effect research showed that:
options: Memory is immune to post-event suggestion
Leading questions can alter eyewitness memories
Emotional events are always remembered accurately
Repressed memories can be reliably recovered
questions.answer: Leading questions can alter eyewitness memories
questions.explanation: Loftus demonstrated that misleading post-event information can become incorporated into eyewitness memory.

questions.question: Spaced practice is superior to massed practice because:
options: It forces repeated retrieval over time strengthening long-term retention
questions.answer: It forces repeated retrieval over time strengthening long-term retention
questions.explanation: Spaced practice distributes learning across time, requiring retrieval from progressively longer intervals, which consolidates memory.

questions.question: The representativeness heuristic often leads to neglect of:
questions.answer: Base rate information (statistical probability)
questions.explanation: People using representativeness ignore statistical base rates when judging probability based on how well something matches a prototype.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Priming is classified as implicit memory because:" is: It influences behavior without conscious awareness.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. It influences behavior without conscious awareness is correct.

questions.question: Vygotsky's scaffolding concept implies that effective instruction should:
options: Always let students discover solutions independently
Provide permanent support for all learning tasks
Target tasks just above the learner's current independent level
questions.answer: Target tasks just above the learner's current independent level
questions.explanation: Scaffolding within the ZPD provides appropriate support for tasks the learner cannot yet do independently, gradually withdrawing help as competence grows.

questions.question: The word-length effect in working memory shows that:
options: Longer words are easier to remember
Fewer long words can be held in the phonological loop than short words
Word length affects only semantic memory
Working memory capacity is unlimited for familiar words
questions.answer: Fewer long words can be held in the phonological loop than short words
questions.explanation: Long words take longer to subvocally rehearse, reducing the number that can be maintained in the phonological loop.

questions.question: Interleaving different types of problems compared to blocked practice results in:
options: Better immediate performance but similar long-term retention
Worse immediate performance but better long-term retention and transfer
No difference in either immediate or long-term performance
Better performance only for simple skills
questions.answer: Worse immediate performance but better long-term retention and transfer
questions.explanation: Interleaving feels harder and produces lower immediate performance, but forces retrieval and discrimination between strategies, improving long-term learning.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Retrograde amnesia involves:" is: Inability to form new long-term memories.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Loss of memories formed before a brain injury.

questions.question: The method of loci memory technique leverages:
options: Spatial mental imagery linked to a familiar route
questions.answer: Spatial mental imagery linked to a familiar route
questions.explanation: The method of loci encodes information by placing mental images along a well-known spatial route, exploiting spatial memory strengths.

questions.question: Prospect theory demonstrates that people are:
options: More motivated to avoid losses than to achieve equivalent gains
Always risk-seeking when facing potential losses
Unaffected by how choices are framed
questions.answer: More motivated to avoid losses than to achieve equivalent gains
questions.explanation: Kahneman and Tversky's prospect theory shows that losses psychologically loom about twice as large as equivalent gains.

questions.question: Expert problem solvers differ from novices primarily in that they:
options: Categorize problems by deep structural principles
Rely more on algorithms than heuristics
questions.answer: Categorize problems by deep structural principles
questions.explanation: Experts represent problems at a deeper, principle-based level while novices focus on surface features, enabling better strategy selection.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Broadbent's filter model of attention proposes that:" is: A selective filter blocks most information based on physical characteristics early in processing.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. A selective filter blocks most information based on physical characteristics early in processing is correct.

questions.question: The dual-coding theory (Paivio) suggests that memory is improved when information is:
options: Encoded in both verbal and visual/imagistic codes
questions.answer: Encoded in both verbal and visual/imagistic codes
questions.explanation: Paivio's dual-coding theory states that information encoded both verbally and visually creates richer, more retrievable memory traces.

questions.question: The incubation effect in problem solving is best explained by:
options: Unconscious processing and forgetting of misleading mental sets
Simple physical recovery from mental fatigue
questions.answer: Unconscious processing and forgetting of misleading mental sets
questions.explanation: Incubation likely involves unconscious processing and forgetting of misleading mental sets, both contributing to eventual insight.

questions.question: A key criticism of the Turing Test as a measure of machine intelligence is:
options: It is too difficult for any machine to pass
Verbal behavior that mimics intelligence does not necessarily involve understanding
It requires machines to have emotional experience
It can only test mathematical ability
questions.answer: Verbal behavior that mimics intelligence does not necessarily involve understanding
questions.explanation: Searle's Chinese Room argument shows that passing the Turing Test does not prove genuine understanding or consciousness.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Human factors (ergonomics) psychology primarily applies cognitive research to:" is: Diagnosing cognitive disorders.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Designing systems that fit human cognitive abilities and limitations.

questions.question: Proactive interference in memory occurs when:
options: New information interferes with old memories
Old information interferes with learning new information
Retrieval cues are absent at test
questions.answer: Old information interferes with learning new information
questions.explanation: Proactive interference: previously learned information makes it harder to learn or remember subsequently learned material.

questions.question: Sapir-Whorf (linguistic relativity) research on color perception suggests:
options: Having more color terms in a language speeds discrimination between those colors
questions.answer: Having more color terms in a language speeds discrimination between those colors
questions.explanation: Research shows that fine-grained linguistic color categories can facilitate faster perceptual discrimination between colors in those categories.

questions.question: The episodic buffer in Baddeley's working memory model was added to explain:
options: Visual manipulation of objects in mental space
Integration of information from multiple sources into coherent episodes
The capacity limits of sensory memory
questions.answer: Integration of information from multiple sources into coherent episodes
questions.explanation: The episodic buffer integrates information from the phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, and long-term memory into unified, multi-modal episodes.
